summary: Joel Spolsky argues that platform success depends entirely on developer adoption. Companies kill their platforms by either failing to recognize they have a platform (treating it as an application) or by being greedy and trying to capture all revenue themselves. The post serves as a brief pointer to a longer essay on platforms.
key_points: Platform success requires massive developer adoption
Making it hard for developers to build on a platform is the fastest way to kill it
Two common platform failures: not recognizing you have a platform, or hoarding revenue
money_quotes: If you want a platform to be successful, you need massive adoption, and that means you need developers to develop for it.
The best way to kill a platform is to make it hard for developers to build on it.
Most of the time, this happens because platform companies either don't know that they have a platform (they think it's an application) or they get greedy (they want all the revenue for themselves.)
key_insight: Platforms die when their owners either fail to recognize they are platforms or refuse to share the economic pie with the developers who make them valuable.
